coredata.dc:title: Photothermal and colorimetric dual mode detection of nanomolar ferric ions in environmental sample based on in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Xin
Gao, Min
Rao, Honghong
Luo, Mingyue
Wang, Hongqiang
An, Pengli
Feng, Tiantian
Lu, Xiaoquan
Xue, Zhonghua
Liu, Xiuhui
coredata.dc:description: Iron ions play a key role in many physiological processes, which can provide feedback for the evaluation of biological systems and environmental processes. New strategies for portable determination of Fe3+ therefore are still in urgent need. Here, through an in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles (PB NPs) in aqueous solution, we developed a bimodal method for photothermal and colorimetric detection of Fe3+. The sensing mechanism is based on the effective oxidation etching of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes induced by Fe3+, accompanied by the in situ generation of PB NPs. It can be attributed to the specific reaction between ferrous ions (Fe2+) from the reduction of Fe3+ and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) in the reaction solution. The in situ produced PB NPs show distinct bare-eye-detectable readouts with highly sensitive colorimetric and photothermal responses and thus can be used for Fe3+ determination. Such colorimetric change signals of characteristic absorbance at 740 nm in the UV–vis spectra showed a sensitive response to Fe3+ with a LOD of 210 nM. Moreover, as a sensitive photothermal probe, PB NPs generated in our Fe3+-enabled reaction system also exhibited a sensitive response to Fe3+ with a LOD of 70 nM. In addition, the standard addition experiments demonstrate our photothermal and colorimetric probe has good applicability for Fe3+ detection in the river water sample. What’s more, the proposed strategy opens a new horizon for affordable detection of metal ions using a common thermometer, and therefore has a great potential for analytical chemistry and some important applications such as environmental monitoring, disease diagnostics and food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes
Prussian blue nanoparticles
Ferric ions
Photothermal
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conditions optimization of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
Colorimetric and photothermal investigation of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
Preparation and characterization of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes
Investigation of colorimetric and photothermal effect of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
Condition optimization of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
Colorimetric quantitation of Fe3+ using the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
Photothermal quantitation analysis of Fe3+ using the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
The practicability of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system for Fe3+ photothermal quantitation analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) the octahedral Au nanocrystals and (b) the Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. (c) TEM image of the single Au–Cu core-shell nanocube. (d) HAADF images of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. Insert: the EDS line scan on two of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. (e) The Moiré fringes from TEM image over the Au core. (f) HR-TEM image of the single Au–Cu core-shell nanocube. Insert: the corresponding Fourier transform (FFT) pattern.

(a) TEM images of the Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes before and after etching by 40 μM Fe3+ addition and 80 μM Fe3+ addition. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra and (c) temperature changes of the reaction solution system. The final concentrations of K3[Fe(CN)6] and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the reaction solutions is 1 mM and 0.0578 mg mL−1, respectively. Temperature monitoring using a common thermometer during the irradiation of an 808 nm laser at a power of 3.5 W for 10 min.

Reaction conditions optimization of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based system. (a) pH. (b) K3[Fe(CN)6] concentrations. (c) Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes concentrations. (d) Reaction time. (e) Reaction temperature.

Photographs (a) and UV–vis absorption spectra (b) of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction solutions obtained from different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range from 0 to 160 μM. (c) Calibration plot of absorbance at 740 nm in UV–vis absorption spectra of the proposed colorimetric reaction solutions vs the Fe3+ concentration (Fe3+/μM). Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

Photographs and absorbance histogram at 740 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction solutions obtained from Fe3+ (80 μM) and different interfering substances (10 mM). Interfering substances: Al3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, K+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+ and Na+. The final concentrations of K3[Fe(CN)6] and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the reaction solutions is 1 mM and 0.0578 mg mL−1, respectively. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

(a) Temperature changes (ΔT) of the different reaction solutions after the irradiation by an 808 nm laser (3.5 W) for 90 s. (b) Temperature monitoring of the different reaction solutions. (c) Relationship between Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes concentration (0.0036–0.058 mg mL−1) and temperature increase (ΔT/°C) of the colorimetric system after irradiation by an 808 nm laser (3.5 W) for different time periods (20–150 s). The final concentrations of Fe3+, Fe2+, K3[Fe(CN)6] and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the reaction solutions are 80 μM, 80 μM, 1 mM and 0.0578 mg mL−1. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

(a) Temperature changes histogram (ΔT) of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction solutions obtained from different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range from 0.625 to 160 μM. (b) Calibration plot of temperature changes (ΔT) of the proposed reaction solutions vs the logarithm of the Fe3+ concentration (Fe3+/μM). (c) Temperature increases (ΔT) of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction solutions obtained from Fe3+ (80 μM) and different interfering substances (10 mM). Interfering substances: Al3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, K+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+ and Na+. The final concentrations of K3[Fe(CN)6] and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the reaction solutions are 1 mM and 0.0578 mg mL−1, respectively. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the photothermal and colorimetric dual mode detection of nanomolar ferric ions based on the in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles.
Photothermal Fe3+ determination results in the real samples (n = 3).
Comparison of the performance of different sensors for Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Photothermal and colorimetric dual mode detection of nanomolar ferric ions in environmental sample based on in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou City University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou City University, Lanzhou, 730070, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xin Xue and Min Gao contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron ions play a key role in many physiological processes, which can provide feedback for the evaluation of biological systems and environmental processes. New strategies for portable determination of Fe3+ therefore are still in urgent need. Here, through an in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles (PB NPs) in aqueous solution, we developed a bimodal method for photothermal and colorimetric detection of Fe3+. The sensing mechanism is based on the effective oxidation etching of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes induced by Fe3+, accompanied by the in situ generation of PB NPs. It can be attributed to the specific reaction between ferrous ions (Fe2+) from the reduction of Fe3+ and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) in the reaction solution. The in situ produced PB NPs show distinct bare-eye-detectable readouts with highly sensitive colorimetric and photothermal responses and thus can be used for Fe3+ determination. Such colorimetric change signals of characteristic absorbance at 740 nm in the UV–vis spectra showed a sensitive response to Fe3+ with a LOD of 210 nM. Moreover, as a sensitive photothermal probe, PB NPs generated in our Fe3+-enabled reaction system also exhibited a sensitive response to Fe3+ with a LOD of 70 nM. In addition, the standard addition experiments demonstrate our photothermal and colorimetric probe has good applicability for Fe3+ detection in the river water sample. What’s more, the proposed strategy opens a new horizon for affordable detection of metal ions using a common thermometer, and therefore has a great potential for analytical chemistry and some important applications such as environmental monitoring, disease diagnostics and food analysis.

Photothermal and colorimetric dual mode detection of nanomolar ferric ions based on in situ generation of prussian blue nanoparticles.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential element in living organism, Ferric ions (Fe3+), often play important roles in many physiological actions such as oxygen transportation [1], hemoglobin synthesis [2], enzyme catalysis [3], cellular metabolism [4], as well as electron transfer [5]. Many efforts have been devoted to demonstrating a close relation between ferric ion and biological systems [6,7]. For instance, the concentration of Fe3+ as a key label for Parkinson’s disease and an important pollutant in environmental monitoring have been well explored [8–11]. New strategies for sensitive and selective Fe3+ levels detection have become significant concerns in biological and environmental [12,13].

Currently, some instrumental-based technologies such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [14], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [15], fluorescent spectrometry [16–18], and electrochemical method [19] have been widely established for Fe3+ quantification. However, most of these analytical techniques usually require complicated sample preparation procedures and sophisticated instrumentations, which further limit their wider applications. Colorimetric sensors, in contrast, have attracted intensive research interest owing to their simplicity, cheapness, practicability, fast response and so forth [20–23], and have been widely used in the areas of visually biological and environmental analysis [24–28], and clinical diagnosis [29,30]. Dual mode detection strategies have recently attracted considerable attention than the single mode method [31–33]. Significantly, colorimetric method has recently been proved to be more promising in the fabrication of newer dual mode analytical system, such as colorimetric/fluorescent dual mode sensing platform, which exhibits extremely sensitive performance for target analysis in biomedical and environmental applications. Because this kind of dual mode sensors not only provide highly sensitive fluorescence assay, but also facilitate the visualization of targets by the unaided eye [34,35]. However, the indispensable complicated construction procedure used for integrating two optical parts together, including color and fluorescence sensing elements are still necessary for these smart dual mode sensors [36–39]. This drawback restricts the practical applications of colorimetric-based dual mode sensors, especially for the utilization of point-of-care testing (POCT) with sensitive readout, rapid measurement, and quantitative detector. Recently, enormous efforts have been exerted to develop straightforward readout-based detection methods for target analyte [40,41], such as height [42], length [43], and temperature [44]. For example, the photothermal analytical platform with a temperature signal-based readout demonstrated convenient, simple and practical merit in different sensing events. Such methods also exhibit excellent simplicity and potential application in POCT and on-site detection fields due to the utilization of a common thermometer reader.

On the basis of the above reason, we attempt to demonstrate an effective strategy for developing a photothermal and colorimetric dual mode detection method, focusing on the determination of nanomolar ferric ions in environmental sample. Previous reports have suggested that PB NPs not only possessed blue-colored optical signal, but also exhibited sensitive temperature readout due to their good photothermal effect in water solution system [45,46]. We herein utilized PB NPs in situ generated from the Fe3+-mediated solution reaction system to develop a simple photothermal/colorimetric dual mode Fe3+ detection method. Compared to the traditional colorimetric/fluorescent dual mode method, our assay exhibits good simplicity because of the unemployable integration of different sensing units. More significantly, our proposed photothermal/colorimetric dual mode system exhibits high detection accuracy and reliability toward ferric ions due to the utilization of the dual mode sensing signal readout.

In this work, we found Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes can effectively capture Fe3+ in aqueous solution, resulting in the oxidization of Cu shell of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. It can be attributed to the well-matched redox potential between Cu0 and Fe3+ species, speculating from the redox potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (0.767 V vs the normal hydrogen electrode, NHE) and Cu2+/Cu0 (0.337 V vs NHE). Meanwhile, such oxidation etching-based modulation of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes surface further leads to the generation of Fe2+ and Cu2+ in the solution system. Then the formed Fe2+ species could combine immediately with the preexisting potassium ferricyanide in the solution, finally resulting in the in situ generation of PB NPs with sensitive color and photothermal signals (Scheme 1 ). Based on this finding, a simple, practical and convenient photothermal/colorimetric dual mode ferric ions detection method was successfully developed, depending on the etching-based surface modulation of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes that mediated by target ferric ions. Significantly, different from the existed dual mode sensors, the signal readouts in our method (e.g. color and temperature) as a naked-eye-detectable signal could be directly and simply detected by the unaided eyes. Therefore, as an effective analytical tool, our strategy shows an obvious advantage in the field of POCT and instrument-free analysis of ferric ions.

HAuCl4 and FeCl3 was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All other metal cations such as Al3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, K+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Na+, and trisodium citrate, hexadecylamine (HDA), HCl, ascorbic acid (AA), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and K3[Fe(CN)6] were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (China). All other chemicals were analytical reagent grade or better. All aqueous solutions were prepared by deionized water (>18.25 MΩ cm).

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on an Agilent UV-8453 spectrophotometer (Agilent Inc., Jpn). The acidity of the buffer solution was monitoring using a Metrohm 632 pH-meter. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a Zeiss electron microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on FEI electron microscopy (TECNAI, G2 F20 S-TWIN, US) equipped with an Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) (Gatan 832, EDAX) operating at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. The concentration of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes materials was measured through ICP-OES (Plasma Quant, PQ 9000, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected with the use of an advance XRD-D8 diffractometer (Germany) with Cu Kα radiation. A diode laser (MDL-H-808-5W-17120005) with output power from 0 to 5 W was purchased from Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd. All photographs were recorded by Canon camera (EOS-70D). A vacuum drying box (Jing Hong DZF-6050, China) and centrifugal machine (Ke Cheng H3–18K, China) were used in the fabrication of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes materials. The water used throughout all experiments is from a purified water system (Youpu UPHW-I-90T, Beijing). A pen-style digital thermometer (model number MITIR-TP677) with a detection range of −50 °C to +300 °C was obtained from a local supermarket.

Octahedral Au nanocrystals and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes were prepared following a hydrothermal synthesis method developed by Huang et al. with a minor modification [47,48]. For the hydrothermal synthesis of octahedral Au nanocrystals, 0.055 g of CTAB dissolved in 9.65 mL of deionized water, 250 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4 solution, and 100 μL of 0.1 M trisodium citrate were added to a glass of bottle with a volume capacity of 20 mL. The sealed glass bottle then was placed in the oven set at the temperature of 110 °C with a time period of 8 h. After that, the glass bottle was removed from the oven and cooled naturally to the room temperature. Here, the obtained solution was monitored by UV–visible absorption spectra to evaluate the synthesized sample. Further, the residual CTAB surfactants on octahedral Au nanocrystals were removed by centrifuging method at 10000 rpm with a time period of 20 min. The supernatant was removed and the precipitate was re-dispersed in deionized water for another cycle of centrifugation. Finally, the as-received nanocrystals were stored in deionized water at 4 °C.

Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes were synthesized by reducing CuCl2 with ascorbic acid in the presence of HDA and octahedral Au nanocrystals under acidic condition [47]. 0.024 g of HDA was first dissolved in 9.0 mL deionized water in a glass vial (20 mL), followed by the addition of 0.1 M CuCl2 solution (200 μL) and octahedral Au nanocrystals solution (500 μL). Next, 0.4 M ascorbic acid solution (300 μL) was added. The total reaction solution volume was well controlled and is 10 mL, and the final HDA concentration is 0.01 M. Then the glass vial was placed in an oven and heated for 1.5 h with temperature set at 100 °C. After the reaction, the glass vial was cooled to room temperature. Removing method of the residual HDA is similar with CTAB surfactants on octahedral Au nanocrystals, where centrifuging was performed at 8500 and 7000 rpm with a time period of 10 min, respectively. The as-obtained nanocubes were finally dispersed in 2 mL of deionized water and stored at 4 °C. The concentration of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the stock solution was identified as 0.578 mg mL−1 through ICP-OES measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions optimization of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes and K3[Fe(CN)6], and etching reaction time, temperature, and pH, have a great influence on the performance of the proposed colorimetric and photothermal system. Therefore, the substrate concentrations of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes, K3[Fe(CN)6], Fe3+, and the pH, the reaction time and temperature of Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation have been investigated, using the highest UV–vis absorbance of the produced PB NPs centered at 740 nm as the monitored signal. The whole optimization conditions were performed after the incubating for 0.5 h with temperature set at 55 °C at different variations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and photothermal investigation of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor the colorimetric and photothermal effect of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations, the substrate solution of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes solution (500 μL), HCl solution (1.0 mL) and potassium ferricyanide (500 μL) in a cuvette (4.0 mL) was incubated for 0.5 h with the temperature of 55 °C. After incubation, the cuvette containing the reacted solution was cooled to room temperature, followed by color, absorbance and photothermal measurements. To study the colorimetric performance of the proposed system, UV–vis spectrometer and digital camera were used for acquiring the UV–vis spectroscopy and color photo, respectively. For the investigation of photothermal effect, the cuvettes with the sample solution were irradiated horizontally with the 808 nm laser for 90 s at a power of 3.5 W. Meantime, a pen-style digital thermometer probe was always in the sample solution to monitor the temperature change in the whole irradiation process. The highest steady temperature value was recorded as the photothermal measurement signal. The final quantitative analysis of target Fe3+ was performed according to the described method above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes were synthesized through a shell-growing procedure using octahedral Au nanocrystal as cores, according to the method previously developed by Huang et al. with a minor modification [47,48]. The formation of octahedral Au nanocrystals was firstly confirmed by TEM (Fig. 1 a), SEM, UV–vis spectrum and EDS (Fig. S1), showing well-dispersed Au nanocrystal with uniform size and octahedral shape armed with an opposite corner distance of ∼41 nm, along with a wine-red color and a characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band centered at 548 nm. These results confirmed the successful preparation of octahedral Au nanocrystals. For the confirmation of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes, we firstly obtained the UV–vis spectrum, which shows an obvious SPR band in the visible spectra range (∼600 nm) with a distinct aubergine color (Fig. S2). It is clear that Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes exhibited an obvious red-shift effect compared to that of octahedral Au nanocrystals, which preliminary indicating a successful coverage of Cu shell on the octahedral Au nanocrystals cores substrate [47]. Additional confirmations were provided by TEM, SEM and XRD, as respectively shown in Fig. 1b, c and Fig. S2. As clearly, Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes show a uniform morphology and composition in size and shape with small Au cores and thin Cu shell, in which an increasing particle size over the octahedral Au nanocrystal are clearly observed. The TEM image of single Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes intuitively shows that the edge length of Au–Cu core-shell nanocube is ∼60 nm, and the Cu shell is ∼18 nm in thickness.

Significantly, the octahedral Au cores are clearly visible inside cubic and right bipyramid particles, which is highly consistent with the literature reports [47]. It was consistent with surface composition characterizations from EDS line scan on two of single Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes, as displayed in Fig. 1d. Where the Au peaks are observable but weak in intensity over the obvious Cu peaks, because the Au cores reside inside the cubes. This further confirms the Au core and Cu shell composition of the proposed Au–Cu core-shell samples. Furthermore, Moiré fringes over the Au core are also observed due to the overlapping lattice fringes of Au and Cu (Fig. 1e). It is noteworthy that high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image shown in Fig. 1f exhibits two phases corresponding to the well-crystallized Cu shells and encapsulated Au, respectively, along with the demonstration of two sets of lattice fringes with interplanar distances of 0.13 and 0.19 nm corresponding to the (220) and (200) crystal planes of cubic Cu phase, respectively. Furthermore, Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (inset of Fig. 1f) indicates a well single crystal-based selected area result, with a zone axis of [100]. Following these observations, HR-TEM image along the [100] orientation of cubic Cu was obtained by us, which presented the angle between the (220) and (200) facets is 46.1°, being in good agreement with the theoretical value (45°). All these results not only indicate the exposed facet of the Au–Cu core-shell cube is {100}, but also corroborate the two distinct crystal orientations of the cube in TEM images were [220] and [200], respectively. Finally, X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Au–Cu core-shell nanoparticles was recorded to confirm above results. As shown in Fig. S2, the diffraction peaks of Au–Cu core-shell nanoparticles lie in 38.27°, 44.60°, 64.28° and 77.55°, respectively, which can be attributed to (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystal planes of pure Au phase with a cubic crystalline structure (JCPDS card No. 01-1172). Additionally, the peaks at 43.47°, 50.67° and 74.67° are ascribed to (111), (200) and (220) facets of cubic Cu phase (JCPDS card No. 01-1242), respectively. All above results strongly suggest the successful preparation of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of colorimetric and photothermal effect of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: TEM, UV–vis absorption and photothermal measurement were used to investigate the optical and photothermal effect properties of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. As shown in Fig. 2 a, a dramatical decrease on the Cu shell size was observed for Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes in the presence of Fe3+ (40 μM) at given reaction conditions, indicating an obvious Fe3+-dependent dimensional and morphological evolution of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. Such shape transformation from cube appearance to sphere-like was greatly accelerated after the addition of high concentration of Fe3+ (80 μM). The cube structure and shape of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes almost disappeared and followed by Au–Cu spherical morphology. This observation suggested that Fe3+ could effectively tune the surface of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes, validating the better etching effect of Fe3+ toward Au–Cu core-shell surface. TEM images demonstrate further demonstrated that octahedra Au cores do not change during the etching process and maintain their stable structure state, indicating the etching reaction mainly happened on the Cu-shell of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. TEM and EDS were also performed and successfully confirmed the PB NPs formation in the proposed blue-colored colorimetric solution (Fig. S3).

To further investigate the colorimetric and photothermal effect of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system, the color, absorbance and temperature changes of the mixture solution in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 2b, in the absence of Fe3+, the system containing Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes and K3Fe(CN)6 displays a faint yellow color (Fig. 2b, insert a), along with an obvious SPR band (∼600 nm) in the visible spectra range (Fig. 2b, curve a) that corresponds to the characteristic optical property of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. Further comparison of the optical properties of the reaction system was conducted by adding Fe3+ as the modulator of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes surface chemistry and the trigger of in situ generation of PB NPs. As clearly, with the addition of 40 μM Fe3+, a broad absorption peak of the reaction system at 740 nm in the UV–vis spectra was observed (Fig. 2b, curve b), along with a.

Faint yellow-to-blue color change (Fig. 2b, insert b), indicating a successful formation of PB NPs in this case [49,50]. More sufficient proofs were obtained by adding high concentration of Fe3+ (80 μM) into the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 2b (curve c and insert c), the reaction system exhibited a dramatically increased absorption at 740 nm in the UV–vis spectra along with an obvious dark-blue color change, suggesting a closely Fe3+-dependent PB NPs generation process of our reaction system. In addition, we found that the reaction system showed a relatively slight temperature increment (black line) after the irradiation by an 808 nm laser (3.5W) for 10 min in the absence of Fe3+ (Fig. 2c), indicating a negligible photothermal activity of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. However, the temperature of the reaction system was greatly increased from 25.5 to 67.9 °C (red line) after the addition of 40 μM Fe3+. Interestingly, a highly augmented temperature from 25.5 to 86.8 °C was clearly observed (blue line) with the addition of 80 μM Fe3+. This means that the addition of Fe3+ not only can mediate the generation of PB NPs, but also can trigger the temperature increment of the reaction system due to the good photothermal effect of the formed PB NPs. It’s worth noting that both the octahedral Au nanocrystals and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes have characteristic UV–vis absorption in the absorbance band range of PBNPs. Furthermore, we also investigated the effects of octahedral Au nanocrystals and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes on our Fe3+-enabled in situ PBNPs generation-based reaction system. Fig. S4 shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum, and the 808 nm laser-driven photothermal effects of the gold nanocrystals and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. As clearly, the absorbance value centered at 740 nm of gold nanocrystals (0.0578 mg·mL-1) and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes (0.0578 mg mL−1) fell into 0.05, and 0.22, respectively. After the irradiation by an 808 nm laser (3.5W) for 10 min, the temperature changes of gold nanocrystals (0.0578 mg mL−1) and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes (0.0578 mg mL−1) only from 25.5 to 28.9 °C and 25.5–33.8 °C, respectively. Such interference in absorbance and temperature are negligible for our system. It strongly suggested our Fe3+-enabled in situ PBNPs generation-based reaction system not only could provide sensitive optical signal change toward Fe3+ species, but also may exhibit good Fe3+-dependent photothermal response. Thus, we believe that our colorimetric system may demonstrate a good feasibility to develop a new photothermal-based detection method toward Fe3+ in solution sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the colorimetric performance of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based analysis system in water, the substrate concentrations of K3Fe(CN)6 and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes, the pH, the reaction time and the temperature of the reaction system were was optimized, respectively. Due to the distinctly characteristic absorbance value at 740 nm in the UV–vis spectra represents the in situ generated PB NPs [51], A 740nm is then chosen as the indicator of PB NPs generation to evaluate the performance of the proposed analysis system. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the reaction system exhibits the pH-dependent absorbance corresponding to the in situ PB NPs generation. The obtained A 740nm reaches a maximum as the pH value located at 2.0 and decrease over the pH range from 3–7, and thus pH 2.0 was selected as the optimal pH in the subsequent experiment. It also indicates that a lower pH medium could efficiently accelerate the generation of PB NPs in our proposed colorimetric system. In this approach, the substrate concentrations of K3Fe(CN)6 and Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes as reaction source substances of PB NPs probe, may directly affect the formation of PB NPs. Fig. 3b shows that A 740nm slightly increased with the increment of K3Fe(CN)6 concentration and reached a platform at 1.0 mM. Therefore, 1.0 mM of K3Fe(CN)6 was selected for the following study. Similarly, the concentration of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes was optimized as well. As shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S5, when the concentration of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes reaches 0.0578 mg mL−1, the A 740nm value tends to be stable. Considering the cost and the practical application of our analysis platform, 0.0578 mg mL−1 of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes was chosen as the optimal concentration. Thus, an amount of 0.0578 mg mL−1 Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes was used in the following study. On the other hand, reaction time and reaction temperature as other crucial factors are very important for our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system. Fig. 3d and e shows the effect of reaction time and reaction temperature of our analysis system on A 740, respectively. It is clear that the value of A 740nm increased dramatically first, and then decreased gradually with the increased reaction time and reaction temperature. A platform of A 740nm can be observed at 30 min (Fig. 3d) and 55 °C (Fig. 3e), respectively. So, 30 min and 55 °C as the optimal reaction time and reaction temperature were chosen for the subsequent study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric quantitation of Fe3+ using the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the color and absorbance change of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations at above optimal condition. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the color of the solution gradually changed from yellow to green and then to blue with the increasing Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 160 μM, and obvious increase on the absorbance of the solution at 740 nm was also found. These results suggest that the added Fe3+ species.

into the mixture solution system could directly induce the in situ generation of PB NPs, thus exhibiting a typical PB NPs-related optical signal with blue color and high absorbance intensity at 740 nm [49,50]. In addition, a linear correlation between A 740 and Fe3+ concentrations obtained in the range from 2.5 to 80 μM (Fig. 4c). The regression equation of A740nm = 0.0098·CFe 3+ + 0.36 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9847 was obtained. The detection limit of Fe3+ at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 is 210 nM, which is comparable and lower than the analytical threshold concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water permitted by the EPA (5 μM) [52]. Therefore, the colorimetric method based on our in situ PB NPs generation reaction system provides a simple and practical way for sensing Fe3+ in different water samples.

Furthermore, the selectivity of our developed colorimetric sensor was also evaluated, and proved that it can be challenged with 125-fold higher concentrations of other metal ions including Al3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, K+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mn2+ and Na+, as shown in Fig. 5 . To study the reproducibility of our developed colorimetric sensing platform, the absorbance at 740 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of eight sample solutions with the same Fe3+ concentration was tested. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the absorbance signal readouts obtained from these samples was less than 5%, implying a good reproducibility of the developed colorimetric sensor (Fig. S6). Significantly, the obtained colorimetric detection results here could be extended into the fabrication of colorimetric logic gate (Fig. S7), further demonstrates its extending and future application in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal quantitation analysis of Fe3+ using the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve photothermal detection of Fe3+ in water sample, the photothermal effect of Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system in the presence of different components was firstly investigated in detail. The temperature of the reaction system during the whole irradiation process was monitored through a pen-style digital thermometer. As seen in Fig. 6 a, no obvious temperature change was observed not only for the water contrast, but also for Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes solution and other control experimental systems. However, with the addition of Fe3+, the solution system of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes exhibit a significant photothermal effect at the given condition, where a gradually increasing temperature from the initial temperature of 25.5 °C–86.8 °C (Fig. 6b, black line) was clearly observed within the increasing time (10 min). The control experiment by adding Fe2+ into the solution of K3[Fe(CN)6] also indicated a comparative photothermal effect compared to the Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system, where the system temperature increased from 25.5 °C to 77.1 °C (Fig. 6b, red line). This means the addition of Fe3+ into our analysis system can induce not only the generation of PB NPs, but also the formation of characteristic temperature-based signal of PB NPs due to their good photothermal effect. It is very similar with that of most photothermal nanomaterials, such as gold and carbon-based nanostructures [53]. Inspired by these findings, we hypothesized that using this in situ PB NPs generation reaction system could construct an affordable and simple photothermal sensor with a thermometer-based readout for selectively and sensitively detecting Fe3+.

To the best of our knowledge, the signal readout plays a key role in the fabrication of the optical and photothermal sensing system. We subsequently evaluated the feasibility of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system as a sensing platform for photothermal detection of Fe3+. Fig. 6c shows the photothermal effect of the PB NPs generation reaction system with different concentrations of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes. As clearly, the temperature of the Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes with different concentrations do not exhibit distinct change upon an irradiation by the 808 nm laser for 60–120 s. Interestingly, in the presence of Fe3+, the temperature of our in situ PB NPs generation reaction system increased dramatically with the increasing concentrations of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes for the different irradiation times from 20 to 150 s. In addition, we found that the temperature increment of the reaction system at an irradiation time of 90 s was proportional to the concentrations of Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes over the range from 0.0036 to 0.0578 mg mL−1. However, the linear relationship of the temperature increment was no longer observed at other irradiation times (less or greater than 90 s). Therefore, 90 s was used as the optimal irradiation time in the subsequent photothermal analysis toward Fe3+.

Subsequently, we studied the analysis performance of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system for the photothermal detection of Fe3+. The temperature changes of the photothermal systems with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ were recorded. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the PB NPs generation-based photothermal assay displayed ultrasensitive temperature changes toward the different concentrations of Fe3+. With the increase of Fe3+ concentrations from 0.625 to 160 μM, the temperature of the photothermal system increases gradually, and was linearly correlated with the logarithm of Fe3+ concentrations with the square of the correlation coefficient of 0.9932 (ΔT = 9.32·Log CFe 3++13.65) (Fig. 7b). The LOD of this photothermal method was 70 nM, a lower value than the analytical threshold concentration of Fe3+ concentration (5 μM) in drinking water permitted by the EPA [52].

Moreover, satisfactory results further confirmed the excellent selectivity of this photothermal method for Fe3+ determination (Fig. 7c), because other interferences do not induce and exhibit obvious temperature change. To study the reproducibility of our developed photothermal sensing platform, the temperature increment of eight sample solutions at the same Fe3+ concentration was tested. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the temperature signal readouts obtained from these samples was less than 5%, implying a good reproducibility of the developed photothermal sensor (Fig. S8). All these results mean the proposed PB NPs generation reaction system as a newer photothermal sensor possesses a good performance for Fe3+ detection with high selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practicability of our Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system for Fe3+ photothermal quantitation analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To test the applicability of our developed photothermal Fe3+ sensor, real water samples collected from the Yellow River water at different regions of Lanzhou (Gansu, China) were tested by using standard addition method. Certain amounts of Fe3+ ions were used to contaminate these water samples, and subsequently tested the temperature change of these contaminated samples system with a simple thermometer-based reader. As shown in Table 1 , all the percent recoveries fell in the range of 93.2%–105%, revealing the desirable applicability of the proposed Fe3+-enabled in situ PB NPs generation-based reaction system for photothermal Fe3+determination.

Moreover, we compared the sensing performance of the developed sensor with other Fe3+ sensors reported elsewhere, the results (Table 2 ) strongly indicate that our assay exhibited much lower LOD for Fe3+ determination. More interestingly, similar with the obtained colorimetric detection results, the photothermal detection event of this approach could be extended into the fabrication of photothermal logic gate (Fig. S9), further suggests its extending application in real samples.

In summary, we developed a new in situ PB NPs generation strategy modulated by Fe3+-mediated Au–Cu core-shell nanocubes-etching principle, and successfully applied it into the dual mode iron ions detection with affordable photothermal and colorimetric method. The proposed approach could enable practical Fe3+ determination with a LOD of 210 nM and 70 nM using bare eyes and an easily accessible thermometer as the quantitative signal reader, respectively. Moreover, as the color and temperature readout for Fe3+ have portability, low cost, wide availability, as well as easy operation, our strategy shows an obvious advantage in the field of POCT and instrument-free analysis of Fe3+ in environmental samples.

Xin Xue: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Min Gao: Writing - original draft. Honghong Rao: Visualization. Mingyue Luo: Investigation. Hongqiang Wang: Software. Pengli An: Software. Tiantian Feng: Data curation. Xiaoquan Lu: Validation. Zhonghua Xue: Writing - review & editing. Xiuhui Liu: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21765013, 21665023 and 21565021), and the Gansu Province Higher Education Research Project (No. 2017A-115).
